title: Legendborn

passage.title: Legendborn (excerpt)
passage.author: By Tracy Deonn
passage.text:
**In this fantasy novel, teenagers descended from [word]’s knights are preparing for a magical war. In the excerpt, sixteen-year-old Bree observes two classmates, Selwyn and Tor, fighting a magical creature in the woods. The creature has just attacked several other teenagers.**

1. A screech splits the air, yanking my attention back to Selwyn and Tor. A hiss. A voice like metal scraping across glass. “*[word]*...”

2. I blink in confusion. *Merlin as in King Arthur?*

3. Selwyn advances on the flickering creature twitching from Tor’s arrow. Five needle-thin points of light appear at the fingertips of his extended hand. He snaps his wrist, and the light spears fly into the ground. The creature screams; Selwyn’s pinned it in place like a butterfly to a board. His low chuckle makes me shiver. “Not just any Merlin.”

4. The creature hisses again in pained rage. “*A [word]!*”

5. A [word] grin spreads across Selwyn’s face. “That’s better.” My heart skips. Mage. *Magic.*

6. “It’s just a small one, Sel.” Tor pouts, another arrow already nocked in her bow.

7. “Doesn’t matter how small it is,” Selwyn—*Sel*—objects. “It shouldn’t be here.”

8. The thing struggles against its restraints. A flapping sound.

9. Sel clucks his tongue. “Why are you here, little *isel*?”

10. He says “isel” with a long “e” on the first syllable—and a __derisive__ sneer.

11. “*Nosy Legendborn!*” The *isel* makes a sniffing sound. “*Nosy trai—*” Sel stomps down on its wing. Hard. The creature screeches.

12. “Enough about us. Why are *you* here?”

13. “*Feeding!*”

14. Sel rolls his eyes. “Yes, we saw that. Found yourself a spark of aggression and *blew on it* until it became a feast. So intent on gorging yourself you didn’t even see us when we were right beneath you. But so far away from campus? You’re a weak, miserable thing. Barely [word]. Surely it’d be easier to feed there, closer to your Gate?”

15. A grating, rhythmic sound comes from the ground where the *isel* lies trapped. It takes a moment for me to recognize the sound as laughter. Sel hears it too; his lips curl back.

16. “Something funny?”

17. “*Yesss*,” the *isel* crows. “*Very funnnnny*...”

18. “Spit it out. We don’t have all night,” Sel warns. “Or should I say *you* don’t have all night? You’re going to die here—or did you miss that, too?”

19. “*Not myyyy Gate*,” it rasps.

20. Sel’s jaw clenches. “What do you mean, not your Gate?”

21. The creature laughs again, the sound [word] and *wrong*. Sel’s eyes flick to Tor. Still aiming at the *isel*, she shakes her head, shrugs. Neither one of them knows what it means. “*Not my Gate. Not my Gate—*”

22. Without warning, Sel clenches his hand into a tight fist in one hard motion. The glowing pins draw together. There’s a quick flash of light and a bone-shaking scream, and the creature’s flickering shape explodes into green dust. 

23. My feet are glued to the earth. *They’re going to find me*, I think, *because I’m too terrified to run.*

24. “There could be more.” Tor pulls her bow to rest. Sel’s head lowers in thought. “Sel?” Silence. “Did you hear me?”

25. His eyes cut to hers. “I heard you.”

26. “Well, we huntin’ or not, Kingsmage?” she huffs.

27. He turns to face the woods opposite my hiding place, tension radiating across his back and shoulders. He comes to a decision. “We’re hunting.” He mutters a word I don’t understand, and the silver smoke from before returns, swirling around the campfire until the flames die, sending the clearing into darkness. “Move out.”

28. I hold my breath, but Tor and Sel don’t turn my way. Instead, they step into the section of the woods he’d been [word]. I wait until I hear their voices [word]. Even without fear of what they’d do if they found me, it takes that long to get my trembling limbs under control. Finally, they’re gone.

29. A beat of silence, two, and the crickets begin singing again. I hadn’t realized they’d stopped. 

30. From a limb overhead, a bird releases a quite uncertain chirp. I exhale in [word]. I’m pretty sure I know how they feel; the *isel* was an impossible monster that somehow fed off humans, but Selwyn is something else…something worse. 

31. Every living thing in the forest had hidden itself from him.

32. I stand there one more beat, still frozen, and then I run. I run as fast as I can through the shadows and don’t look back.

questions.question_content: **What is the best definition of *derisive*, as it is used in paragraph 10?**

note: KA serves 1/4 questions to anonymous users. Passage and sample question captured for skeleton generation.
